attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a one-month history of painless neck swelling. He reports weight loss of 4 kg, palpitations, excessive sweating, and intermittent hoarseness. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 75/min, blood pressure 125/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is a non-tender, firm 2 cm nodule in the left thyroid region that moves on swallowing. A firm, indistinct swelling is palpable in the left supraclavicular fossa.

Routine blood tests are within normal limits. A radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) scan shows increased uptake in the left thyroid nodule with normal to slightly decreased uptake in the surrounding thyroid tissue. No evidence of distant metastases is noted.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following features is most suggestive of malignancy?
options: The physical character of the thyroid nodule
The hot nodule on radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU)
attributes.explanation:
In this clinical scenario, several features raise suspicion for thyroid malignancy. However, the presence of cervical lymphadenopathy is the most suggestive of malignancy, particularly in the context of a thyroid nodule. The firm, indistinct swelling palpable in the left supraclavicular fossa likely represents cervical lymphadenopathy. This finding is highly concerning for metastatic spread of thyroid cancer, most commonly papillary thyroid carcinoma, to regional lymph nodes.

The physical character of the thyroid nodule can be an important indicator of malignancy. Characteristics such as firmness, fixation to adjacent tissues, and size greater than 4 cm are concerning. In this case, while the nodule is described as firm, it moves on swallowing, suggesting it is not fixed. The 2 cm size is not particularly large for a thyroid nodule. Therefore, while the physical examination is important, it is less specific for malignancy than cervical lymphadenopathy in this scenario.

A hot nodule on radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) scan indicates increased uptake of radioactive iodine, which is typically associated with benign, hyperfunctioning nodules such as toxic adenomas. While malignancy is not completely ruled out in hot nodules, it is less common. Therefore, this finding is actually less suggestive of malignancy compared to other features in this case.

Intermittent hoarseness can be a concerning symptom if it's due to recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement by a thyroid malignancy. However, given this patient's significant smoking history, the hoarseness could also be attributed to chronic laryngeal irritation from smoking. While it shouldn't be dismissed, it's less specific for thyroid malignancy than cervical lymphadenopathy.

The recent onset of palpitations, along with weight loss and excessive sweating, suggests hyperthyroidism. While these symptoms can occur with some thyroid malignancies (particularly medullary thyroid cancer), they are more commonly associated with benign conditions such as toxic adenoma or Graves' disease. Therefore, palpitations are the least specific for malignancy among the options presented.

In conclusion, while all these features warrant further investigation, the presence of cervical lymphadenopathy is the most specific indicator of potential thyroid malignancy in this clinical scenario. The next steps in management would likely include fine-needle aspiration biopsy of both the thyroid nodule and the suspicious lymph node, as well as a comprehensive neck ultrasound.
learningPoints: Physical examination findings that should raise concern for malignancy include thyroid nodules larger than 4 cm in size (approximately 19% risk for malignancy), firmness on palpation, fixation to adjacent tissues, cervical lymphadenopathy, and vocal cord paralysis.
The presence of cervical lymphadenopathy in the context of a thyroid nodule is highly suggestive of malignancy, particularly papillary thyroid carcinoma.
references: Boucai L, Zafereo M, Cabanillas ME. Thyroid Cancer: A Review. JAMA 2024; 33:425-35. Available at: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2814532
